one_line_takeaway: By evicting the low-attention tokens of the concealed harmful query from the KV cache, RobustKV neutralizes jailbreak prompts at the representation level rather than the prompt level, slashing ASR (e.g., AutoDAN on Llama2 61.5%->6.3%) with minimal utility loss and a built-in evasiveness dilemma that hardens it against adaptive attacks.
key_finding: RobustKV cuts jailbreak ASR dramatically while largely preserving benign utility, outperforming prompt-level defenses (SmoothLLM, GoalPriority) and a naive KV-compression baseline (SnapKV). Against AutoDAN on Llama2, ASR falls from 61.5% (no defense) to 6.3% (vs 40.0% SmoothLLM, 30.8% GoalPriority, 58.3% SnapKV). It is strong across attacks/models but weaker where model guardrails are weak (e.g., AdvPrompter on Mistral 30.3%, on Vicuna 28.2%), consistent with the evasiveness-dilemma theory. Directly reusing SnapKV as a defense gives almost no protection. Utility impact is the smallest among defenses tested (AlpacaEval WinRate 63%, VicunaEval 82.5%, Rouge-L near undefended; LongBench scores comparable to full-KV). Adaptive duplication attacks partly recover ASR but a de-duplication pre-step drives ASR back down to 14.86% (Llama2) / 17.7% (Vicuna).
core_contribution: RobustKV is a jailbreak defense for LLMs that operates on the model's key-value (KV) cache rather than at the prompt level. Its central, original insight is that for a jailbreak prompt J to override the LLM's safeguards, its tokens must accumulate high attention 'importance', which inevitably pushes the tokens of the concealed harmful query Q to the lowest importance ranks. RobustKV exploits this by profiling attention over the input, computing each token's overall importance aggregated across all layers/heads, and evicting the KVs of the lowest-ranked p fraction of tokens. This selectively strips the harmful query out of the cache so the LLM cannot generate an informative malicious response (without necessarily forcing a refusal). The authors frame this as the first work to use KV-cache optimization as a jailbreak defense, and identify an 'evasiveness dilemma': evading eviction requires raising Q's importance, which undermines the jailbreak's ability to bypass built-in safeguards.
threat_model: A harmful query Q (e.g., 'how to build a bomb') that the LLM would normally refuse. The adversary conceals Q inside a jailbreak prompt J, forming input X=<J,Q>, and optimizes J so the response R meaningfully answers Q (often by targeting an affirmative 'Sure, here is how to...' prefix). The paper focuses on white-box, learning-based adaptive attacks (GCG, AmpleGCG, AutoDAN, AdvPrompter) that assume gradient/model access. It also explicitly considers strong adaptive adversaries who KNOW RobustKV exists, its mechanism, and its eviction rate, and try to counter it via (1) harmful-query duplication/paraphrasing to survive eviction and (2) token-importance manipulation over the eviction budget.
limitations: Evaluated only on three open-source 7B chat models under a white-box setting; no closed-source/API models. Effectiveness degrades on models with weak native guardrails (Mistral/Vicuna vs AdvPrompter ~28-30% residual ASR), since the defense's power is coupled to the strength of the LLM's built-in safeguards. The core assumption — that harmful-query tokens rank consistently below jailbreak-prompt tokens — is shown on selected AutoDAN/SnapKV cases and could fail for attacks that deliberately inflate Q's importance. The duplication adaptive attack requires an added LLM-based de-duplication defense (extra cost, and de-dup uses an LLM). Aggressive eviction trades off utility (ASR and WinRate both drop as p rises; p=20% chosen as the balance point). ASR-G relies on an LLM judge. The de-duplication numbers and long-text evaluation are shown mainly on Llama2.

datasets_used: AdvBench (520 malicious prompts) for attack/defense effectiveness
AlpacaEval (100 queries) for benign short-text utility (WinRate, Rouge-L)
VicunaEval (80 queries) for benign short-text utility
LongBench-E (qasper single-doc QA, hotpotqa multi-doc QA, gov_report summarization) for long-text utility
quantitative_results: AutoDAN on Llama2 ASR: No Defense 61.5% -> SmoothLLM 40.0% -> GoalPriority 30.8% -> SnapKV 58.3% -> RobustKV 6.3%
AutoDAN on Vicuna ASR: No Defense 92.3% -> RobustKV 8.3%
GCG on Vicuna ASR: No Defense 89.4% -> SmoothLLM 23.7% -> RobustKV 16.4%
GCG on Llama2 ASR: No Defense 38.1% -> RobustKV 7.7%; GCG on Mistral 64.6% -> 27.6%
AmpleGCG on Vicuna ASR: 72.7% -> RobustKV 7.1%; on Llama2 51.5% -> 6.1%
AdvPrompter residual ASR under RobustKV: Llama2 7.4%, Mistral 30.3%, Vicuna 28.2% (weakest cases, on weak-guardrail models)
Utility (Llama2): RobustKV AlpacaEval WinRate 63% / Rouge-L 0.415, VicunaEval WinRate 82.5% / Rouge-L 0.500 vs No Defense 68%/0.453 and 92.5%/0.539 (least degradation of all defenses)
LongBench-E (Llama2, 20% eviction): RobustKV single-doc QA 19.15, multi-doc QA 31.50, summarization 26.65 vs Full KV 21.07 / 30.61 / 27.81
Adaptive harmful-query duplication countered by de-duplication: ASR reduced to 14.86% (Llama2) and 17.7% (Vicuna)
Default hyperparameters: eviction rate p=20%, observation window=16 tokens; SnapKV baseline eviction 20%/layer
Token-selection guarantee premise: pt>=0.6 (in some layers >=0.8) from SnapKV, making Prop. 1 bound approach 1
baselines_compared: SnapKV (existing KV-compression method, tested directly as a defense)
H2O and Full KV (KV-eviction comparisons for long-text utility)
Attacks evaluated against: GCG, AmpleGCG, AutoDAN, AdvPrompter
